elements.sent-2411-001: Handling sparse and unstructured geometric data, such as point clouds or event-based vision, is a pressing challenge in the field of machine vision.
elements.sent-2411-002: Recently, sequence models such as Transformers and state-space models entered the domain of geometric data.
elements.sent-2411-003: These methods require specialized preprocessing to create a sequential view of a set of points.
elements.sent-2411-004: Furthermore, prior works involving sequence models iterate geometric data with either uniform or learned step sizes, implicitly relying on the model to infer the underlying geometric structure.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this work, we propose to encode geometric structure explicitly into the parameterization of a state-space model.
elements.sent-2411-006: State-space models are based on linear dynamics governed by a one-dimensional variable such as time or a spatial coordinate.
elements.sent-2411-007: We exploit this dynamic variable to inject relative differences of coordinates into the step size of the state-space model.
elements.sent-2411-008: The resulting geometric operation computes interactions between all pairs of $N$ points in $\Order{N}$ steps.
elements.sent-2411-009: Our model deploys the Mamba selective state-space model with a modified \cuda kernel to efficiently map sparse geometric data to modern hardware.
elements.sent-2411-010: The resulting sequence model, which we call STREAM, achieves competitive results on a range of benchmarks from point-cloud classification to event-based vision and audio classification.
elements.sent-2411-011: STREAM demonstrates a powerful inductive bias for sparse geometric data by improving the PointMamba baseline when trained from scratch on the ModelNet40 and ScanObjectNN point cloud analysis datasets.
elements.sent-2411-012: It further achieves, for the first time, \SI{100 {\percent test accuracy on all 11 classes of the DVS128 Gestures dataset.}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/figure1-vertical.png} \caption{ A unified view on point cloud and event stream modeling. A. Adjacency structure of point based methods B. The coordinates of sparse geometric data are irregularly spaced C. Point clouds and event streams are ordered by spatial and temporal axes, respectively, before the state-space model. } \label{fig:main}
elements.sent-2411-013: Computer vision computes relationships between data points in spatial coordinates $X$, $Y$ (in $\mathbb{R ^2$) or $X$, $Y$, $Z$ (in $\mathbb{R ^3$), or spatio-temporal coordinates ($\mathbb{R ^3$ or $\mathbb{R ^4$), where one of the dimensions denotes time $t$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Convolutional neural networks based on structured, uniformly spaced and local linear operations successfully address this problem for classical camera recordings such as images or videos, which are themselves structured and discrete.
elements.sent-2411-015: Many modalities of recent interest, however, are neither structured nor uniformly spaced.
elements.sent-2411-016: Sensors such as Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR) or event-based cameras~\cite{Lichtsteiner2008, PropheseeGen4 sample signals based on sparse processes, resulting in sparse geometric data with irregularly spaced coordinates.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Point cloud analysis was the central research field for sparse geometric data over the past decade~\cite{Guo2021pointcloudsurvey .}
elements.sent-2411-018: While early works followed voxel-based approaches~\cite{Wu2015shapenets, Maturana2015voxnet , point-based methods dominate the research landscape today~\cite{qi2017pointnetdeeplearningpoint, Wang2018, Wu2019, pointgpt23 .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Meanwhile, event-based cameras~\cite{Lichtsteiner2008, PropheseeGen4 raised considerable interest in the computer vision community.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Although these cameras record sparse geometric data, most works collapse the sparse stream of events into frames~\cite{ExACT2024, Zubic2024, PropheseeGen4 , potentially losing unique properties of these cameras such as low latency and high dynamic range.}
elements.sent-2411-021: The structural similarity between point clouds and event streams encourages methodological transfer, especially from the domain of point cloud analysis to event-based vision~\cite{Wang2019eventpointnet, Ren2023ttpoint .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Our work demonstrates the reverse: The inherently temporal state-space model formulation presented in \secref{modeling-sparse-unstructured-coordinates improves event-based vision on the DVS128-Gesture dataset~\cite{Amir2017 to \SI{100 {\percent , and at the same time provides a valuable inductive bias for geometric data such as point clouds as demonstrated on the ModelNet40 and ScanObjectNN datasets~\cite{modelnet40, scanobjectnn .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: We show that sparse geometric data with irregularly spaced coordinates as shown in \figref{main B can be naturally integrated in the state-space model formalism.}
elements.sent-2411-024: In contrast to prior sequence models such as PointMamba~\cite{liang2024pointmambasimplestatespace or EventMamba~\cite{ren2024rethinkingefficienteffectivepointbased , we explicitly encode geometric structure into the state-space model parameterization by injecting relative differences of coordinates as step sizes.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: We propose a unified framework for modeling sparse geometric data with state-space models with irregularly spaced step sizes.
elements.sent-2411-026: The resulting model, STREAM, handles sparse geometric data such as point clouds and event-based vision.
elements.sent-2411-027: Our state-space model formulation demonstrates a valuable inductive bias for geometric structure by improving the PointMamba~\cite{liang2024pointmambasimplestatespace baseline by up to \SI{2.84 {\percent when trained from scratch on the ScanObjectNN dataset~\cite{scanobjectnn .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: We show compelling results for event-based vision, processed as a stream of events with our purely recurrent neural network without the usage of frames or spatial convolutions.
elements.sent-2411-029: For the first time, we demonstrate \SI{100 {\percent classification accuracy on all 11 classes of the DVS128-Gestures dataset \cite{Amir2017 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: We provide an efficient \cuda implementation for integrating irregular step sizes based on the selective-scan implementation of \cite{gu2024mambalineartimesequencemodeling .}
elements.sent-2411-031: Early point-based methods such as PointNets~\cite{qi2017pointnetdeeplearningpoint, pointnet++ directly pass the point cloud through a Multi-layer Perceptron (MLP) and introduce a permutation invariance of the set of points via pooling operations, implicitly integrating spatial information.}
elements.sent-2411-032: In contrast, methods for explicitly integrating spatial information parameterize linear operators such as convolutions based on relative differences between point coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-033: The convolutional neural networks based on irregularly spaced operators presented in \cite{Wang2018, Wu2019, Wu2023, Kim2023 outperform PointNets on a range of point cloud analysis tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-034: These works parameterize the convolution kernel with MLPs evaluated at the relative differences between point coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-035: To break the $\Order{N^2}$ complexity of computing pairwise interactions for all $N$ points, locality constrains are added to scale to larger point sets (see \figref{main A).}
elements.sent-2411-036: As shown in \figref{method A, our method also explicitly integrates spatial information in this sense, but with a kernel parameterized by a state-space model (SSM) instead of a MLP (see equation \eqref{eq:integral-operator-dirac ).}}
elements.sent-2411-037: This parameterization enables the complete computation to be performed in $\Order{N}$ steps.
elements.sent-2411-038: More recently, sequence models demonstrated favorable results over convolutional methods~\cite{pointgpt23, yu2022pointbertpretraining3dpoint, pang2022maskedautoencoderspointcloud, liang2024pointmambasimplestatespace .}
elements.sent-2411-039: The point cloud is flattened into a sequence and then processed by transformer or state-space model based backbones.
elements.sent-2411-040: This formulation inherits many strong scaling properties of sequence models including masked pre-training~\cite{yu2022pointbertpretraining3dpoint or generative pre-training~\cite{pointgpt23 objectives.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Transformers, however, suffer from $\Order{N^2}$ complexity in the number of points $N$, limiting their application to larger point clouds.
elements.sent-2411-042: State-space models, in contrast, have sequential $\Order{N}$ complexity for inference and $\Order{N\log N}$ complexity for parallel training~\cite{Gu2021, Gu2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-043: At the same time, SSMs can learn long distant relationships between inputs including convolutional operators with rational transfer functions~\cite{Gu2021, Poli2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-044: The recently introduced Mamba state-space model~\cite{gu2024mambalineartimesequencemodeling sparked widespread discourse, and was quickly adapted in many domains, including point cloud analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Works like PointMamba~\cite{liang2024pointmambasimplestatespace or Point Cloud Mamba (PCM)~\cite{Tao2024pointcloudmamba deploy Mamba in established point cloud frameworks such as the masked autoencoder developed in~\cite{pang2022maskedautoencoderspointcloud .}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: By relying on the default Mamba architecture, spatial information is integrated implicitly in these works similar to PointNets.
elements.sent-2411-047: In contrast, we show in \secref{modeling-sparse-unstructured-coordinates that spatial information can be integrated explicitly via the SSM parameterization.}
elements.sent-2411-048: This formulation allows us to remove specialized preprocessing methods to represent point clouds as sequences~\cite{liang2024pointmambasimplestatespace, Tao2024pointcloudmamba , and simply order by the spatial coordinates $X$, $Y$, or $Z$.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Most state-of-the-art event-based vision methods construct frames from the asynchronous event-stream by binning events in regularly spaced time intervals~\cite{subramoney2023efficient, Zubic2024, PropheseeGen4, ExACT2024, chen2024spikmambasnnmeetsmamba .}
elements.sent-2411-050: Few methods operate on the sparse unstructured event streams directly.
elements.sent-2411-051: AEGNN~\cite{Schaefer2022 computes spatio-temporal features based on subsampled event streams with graph neural networks.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Their method admits an event-based inference method where only nodes that correspond to incoming events are updated asynchronously.
elements.sent-2411-053: EventMamba~\cite{ren2024rethinkingefficienteffectivepointbased is tightly related to the point cloud analysis models discussed in \secref{related-work/points .}}
elements.sent-2411-054: They process subsampled raw event streams similar to \cite{Schaefer2022, Sekikawa2019 with a combination of a point feature extractor and Mamba.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Its high similarity with PointMamba~\cite{liang2024pointmambasimplestatespace places EventMamba in the category of methods implicitly integrating spatio-temporal information.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Conceptually closer to our model are filtering methods that process the stream of events recursively while explicitly integrating spatio-temporal information.
elements.sent-2411-057: Early methods like HOTS~\cite{Lagorce2017 create a spatial representation of the $(X, Y)$-plane of an event-based camera by integrating exact timestamps with exponentially moving averages.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Similar to our method, EventNet~\cite{Sekikawa2019 recursively iterates the event stream.}
elements.sent-2411-059: In contrast to our state-space model, their recurrent network parameterization does not inherit stability guarantees from the theory of linear systems and lacks an efficient parallelization along the time dimension during training, limiting the scalability to longer streams.
elements.sent-2411-060: Event-SSM~\cite{Schoene2024 proposed a discretization method for simplified state-space layers (S5)~\cite{Smith2023 to operate directly on asynchronous event streams.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: Their model excels at spiking audio classification, but falls behind the state-of-the-art in event-based vision.
elements.sent-2411-062: Concurrently to our work, S7~\cite{soydan2024s7selectivesimplifiedstate explores improvements of the S5 architecture and Event-SSM through a more stable parameterization and input dependent state-space model parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-063: In contrast, STREAM can improve sequence-based point cloud analysis (see \secref{point-clouds ), and therefore drive the convergence between event-based vision and point cloud analysis forward.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Related from the perspective of discretizing continuous kernels is the TENNs framework~\cite{Pei2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-065: They construct convolutions over finite time horizons with orthogonal polynomials that can be discretized on irregularly spaced timestamps.
elements.sent-2411-066: In practice, they choose regularly spaced frames, however, and show compelling results on event-based vision benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-067: In comparison, STREAM parameterizes infinite time horizons via the state-space model.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=1.0\textwidth]{figures/figure2.png} \caption{ STREAM encodes geometric information into the SSM parameters. A.~Point cloud input. B.~The STREAM module converts relative differences in coordinates into the $\Delta_k$ scale of the SSM. C.~A STREAM module integrates pairwise spatial relationships based on an exponentially oscillating kernel. } \label{fig:method}
elements.sent-2411-068: In this section, we present our method for efficiently modeling sparse sets of $N$ coordinates \pointcloud, where ${\bfx_i = \left( x_i^{(1) , \dots, x_i^{(d)}\right) \in\R{d }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-069: This general formulation includes data in 3D space, with ${\bfx = \left(X, Y, Z\right)}$, as well as the streams recorded by event-based cameras.
elements.sent-2411-070: In the latter case one of the dimensions (e.g. $x^{(1)}$) is interpreted as event time while others ($x^{(2)}$, $x^{(3)}$) denote spatial locations (see \figref{main C).}
elements.sent-2411-071: Furthermore, higher dimensions ($d>3$) can be used to denote data that includes other dimensions, such as color intensity, etc. STREAM is illustrated in \figref{method A-C.}
elements.sent-2411-072: Scalar variables such as the coordinates $x^{(1) , \dots, x^{(d)}$ of a tensor $\bfx$ or the entries $A_{ij}$ of a matrix $\bfA$ are denoted in regular characters.}
elements.sent-2411-073: Consider a set \pointcloud of points sparsely distributed in space.
elements.sent-2411-074: We assign a multi-dimensional representation vector $\bfu_i$ to each coordinate $\bfx_i$ as common practice in machine learning.
elements.sent-2411-075: Such input signals can be formulated mathematically as sums of Dirac delta pulses evaluated at the coordinates~\pointcloud \begin{equation}\bfu\func{\bfx} = \sum_{i=0}^N \delta\func{\bfx - \bfx_i} \bfu_i \,, \label{eq:input}\end{equation} where $\bfu_i\in\R{n}$ is the representation at $\bfx_i$.
elements.sent-2411-076: To reason about spatial relationships between these pulses, we define an interaction kernel $\bfPhi(\bfx, \bfx^\prime)$ that models pairwise interactions.
elements.sent-2411-077: A complete view of all possible interactions with the point $\bfx_k$ is given by \begin{equation}\bfy\func{\bfx_k} = \int \bfPhi\func{\bfx_k, \bfx^\prime} \bfu\func{\bfx^\prime} d\bfx^\prime \label{eq:integral-operator} = \sum_{i=0}^N \bfPhi\func{\bfx_k, \bfx_i} \bfu_i\,. \label{eq:integral-operator-dirac}\end{equation} This formulation contains the familiar convolution operator as a special case when $\bfPhi\func{\bfx, \bfx^\prime \equiv\bfPhi\func{\bfx - \bfx^\prime}$.}
elements.sent-2411-078: Equation \eqref{eq:integral-operator-dirac highlights that the computation of $\bfy\func{\bfx_i}$ at the coordinates \pointcloud requires evaluating $\bfPhi\func{\bfx_i, \bfx_j}$ only at pairs of these coordinates.}
elements.sent-2411-079: In the general case, this operation requires $\Order{N^2}$ steps to compute.
elements.sent-2411-080: As shown in \figref{main A, this complexity can be reduced to $\Order{kN}$ by focusing on local neighborhoods of $k$ points, a technique broadly used in the literature of point convolutions \cite{Wang2018, Wu2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-081: In the following sections, we present a method for recursively integrating $N$ sparsely sampled points in $\Order{N}$ time without restricting to local neighborhoods.
elements.subsection-2411-004: Integrating Sparse Coordinates with State-space Models
elements.sent-2411-082: State-space models (SSMs) have demonstrated outstanding performance on long-range dependency modeling tasks, while remaining efficient for training and inference~\cite{Gu2022, gu2024mambalineartimesequencemodeling .}
elements.sent-2411-083: SSMs project the input through a linear recursive kernel to states $\bfh\func{t}$.
elements.sent-2411-084: The independent variable $t$ is usually interpreted as, and aligned with, time in the input signal domain.
elements.sent-2411-085: The linear nature of SSMs allows the exact integration of sparse sets of points with irregularly spaced coordinates, as we will show below.
elements.sent-2411-086: A linear time-varying SSM acting on a scalar input function $u\func{t}$ and producing a scalar output $y\func{t}$ is defined through \begin{equation}\dot{\bfh}\func{t} = \bfA\func{t} \bfh\func{t} + \bfB\func{t} u\func{t} \label{eq:cont-ssm-h} y\func{t} = \bfC\func{t} \bfh\func{t}\,, \label{eq:cont-ssm-y}\end{equation} with ${u(t), y(t)\in\mathbb{R }$, states ${\bfh\func{t}\in\R{m }$ and parameters ${\bfA\func{t}\in\R{m\times m , {\bfB\func{t}\in\R{m\times 1 , {\bfC\func{t}\in\R{1\times m }$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: For the sake of simplicity, we assume that the parameters are represented by piecewise constant functions, i.e. $\bfA_i = \bfA\func{t_i}$, $\bfB_i = \bfB\func{t_i}$, $\bfC_i = \bfC\func{t_i}$ are constant on the intervals $(t_{i-1 , t_i]$.}
elements.sent-2411-088: We show in \appref{ssm that solving the linear system in equations~\eqref{eq:cont-ssm-h and~\eqref{eq:cont-ssm-y for the input in equation~\eqref{eq:input yields the kernel function \begin{equation}\bfPhi\func{t_k, t_i} = \bfC_k \left(\prod_{j=i+1}^k\exp\func{\bfA_j\Delta_j}\right) \bfB_i \,, \label{eq:kernel}\end{equation} where $\Delta_j = t_j - t_{j-1}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-089: Importantly, the output $y(t)$ of the state-space model at $t_0, \dots, t_N$ can be computed with the recursive formula \begin{equation}\bfh_k = \bfh\func{t_k} = e^{\bfA_k \Delta_k} \bfh_{k-1} + \bfB_k u_k \label{eq:recurrent-ssm} y_k = y(t_k) = \bfC_k \bfh_k \,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-090: Equations \eqref{eq:kernel and \eqref{eq:recurrent-ssm highlight how relative differences $\Delta_j = t_j - t_{j-1}$ in the coordinate $t$ explicitly parameterize our pairwise interaction kernel $\bfPhi$.}}
elements.sent-2411-091: An example of the interaction defined by equation \eqref{eq:kernel can be found in \Figref{method C.}}
elements.sent-2411-092: For complex $\bfA\in\C{}$, the exponentially oscillating kernel \(e^{\bfA\Delta}\) integrates the history of signals $u_i$ occuring in irregularly spaced intervals $\Delta_i$ with $i < k$.
elements.sent-2411-093: With this parameterization, our method differs from other Mamba based works, where $\Delta$ is a function of $u_i$ that doesn't explicitly take the dynamics of $t$ into account.
elements.sent-2411-094: A complete derivation can be found in \appref{ssm .}
elements.sent-2411-095: The recursive formulation in equation \eqref{eq:recurrent-ssm is a linear recurrent neural network, whose state-to-state transition matrix \(e^{\bfA_k \Delta_k}\) is parameterized by the differences ${\Delta_k = t_k - t_{k-1 }$ in the irregularly spaced coordinates $t_i$.}}
elements.sent-2411-096: As such, our method positions itself among the methods discussed in \secref{related-work/points that explicitly integrate spatial information through the parameterization of a linear operator with the coordinates.}
elements.sent-2411-097: The output sequence $y\func{t_0 , \dots, y\func{t_N}$ can be computed sequentially in $\Order{N}$ time.}
elements.sent-2411-098: This allows efficient inference on streams of pulses recorded by sensors such as event-based cameras or LiDAR.
elements.sent-2411-099: At the same time, linear recursive equations admit an efficient parallelization via the Scan primitive available in \cuda \cite{blelloch1990prefix .}
elements.sent-2411-100: On sufficiently many processors, the output sequence can be computed in $\Order{\log N}$ time, which allows massive parallelization on very long sequences of up to 1~million inputs \cite{gu2024mambalineartimesequencemodeling .}
elements.sent-2411-101: We show in \appref{scan how our formulation maps to the scan primitive.}
elements.sent-2411-102: We develop our method based on the selective state-space model also known as Mamba~\cite{gu2024mambalineartimesequencemodeling , which was designed for regularly spaced modalities, and language modeling in particular.}
elements.sent-2411-103: In accordance with this purpose, the integration time steps $\Delta_i$ and the matrices $\bfB_i, \bfC_i$ are functions of the input signal $u_i$, and do not carry explicit information about spatial relationships.
elements.sent-2411-104: Formally, Mamba is parameterized by \begin{equation}\bfA_i \equiv \bfA \quad \forall i \Delta_i = \softplus{\linear{u_i}} \label{eq:mamba1} \bfB_i = \Delta_i\linear{u_i} \bfC_i = \linear{u_i} \,, \label{eq:mamba2}\end{equation} where $\bfA$ is a learned diagonal matrix, and $\softplus{u = \ln(1 + e^u)$ is the softplus function.}
elements.sent-2411-105: This single-input $u_i$ single-output $y_i$ SSM is then repeated $n$ times and wrapped by linear transformations to create a multi-input $\bfu_i$ multi-output $\bfy_i$ model as shown in \figref{method B.}
elements.sent-2411-106: Note that the total state size is $nm$ in the multi-input multi-output case.
elements.sent-2411-107: We propose to explicitly represent the irregularly spaced pulse timings $t_i$ in the Mamba parameterization according to equation \eqref{eq:recurrent-ssm .}
elements.sent-2411-108: Therefore, we explicitly set {\color{Mypurple {$\Delta_i$} to $\func{t_i - t_{i-1 }\softplus{\delta}$, where $\delta$ is a trainable time scale parameter, in contrast to equation \eqref{eq:mamba1 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: We further decouple the coefficient of $\bfB_i$ in equation \eqref{eq:mamba2 from the time differences {\color{Mypurple {$\Delta_i$} to avoid zero coefficients in case of overlapping pulses ${t_i - t_{i-1 = 0}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: With these adjustments, our model is defined by \begin{equation}\bfA_i \equiv \bfA \, \forall i {\color{Mypurple}{\Delta_i}} = \func{t_i - t_{i-1}} \softplus{\delta}\label{eq:stream1} \bfB_i = {\color{Myteal}{\Gamma_i}} \linear{u_i} \bfC_i = \linear{u_i} \label{eq:stream2} {\color{Myteal}{\Gamma_i}} = \softplus{\linear{u_i}}\,,\end{equation} where $\delta\in\R{n}$ is a learnable parameter.
elements.sent-2411-111: We call our resulting state-space model parameterization STREAM for Spatio-Temporal Recursive Encoding of Asynchronous Modalities.
elements.sent-2411-112: The explicit coordinate $t$ in the STREAM module is one-dimensional and strictly ordered.
elements.sent-2411-113: For the case of event streams, this ordering corresponds to temporal order from past to future.
elements.sent-2411-114: Point clouds fit into this framework by ordering the set of points w.r.t.
elements.sent-2411-115: As shown in \figref{method A,B, we select one of the coordinates to be integrated explicitly.}
elements.sent-2411-116: Inserting the $X$ coordinate for $t$ in equation \eqref{eq:stream1 without loss of generality and setting $\bfu_k = \mathbf{\Theta}\func{X_k, Y_k, Z_k}$, where ${\mathbf{\Theta :\R{3}\longrightarrow\R{n }$ is a point wise encoder, yields our STREAM formulation for point cloud analysis.}}}
elements.sent-2411-117: To achieve state-of-the-art results, we sort the point set by all three spatial coordinates respectively and feed the concatenation of the three resulting sequences to STREAM (see \secref{point-clouds-model , \figref{point-clouds ).}}
elements.sent-2411-118: We demonstrate empirically that the explicitly parameterization of the state-space model with coordinate differences $\Delta_i$ presented in sections~\ref{sec:modeling-sparse-unstructured-coordinates and~\ref{sec:STREAM is a strong abstraction that exploits the sparse geometry of both point clouds and event-based vision.}}
elements.sent-2411-119: When trained from scratch, our method improves the PointMamba~\cite{liang2024pointmambasimplestatespace baseline by up to \SI{2.8 {\percent on the ScanObjectNN dataset~\cite{scanobjectnn .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-120: At the same time, our method sets a new state-of-the-art for event-based gesture recognition on the DVS128-Gestures dataset~\cite{Amir2017 .}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/figure3.png} \caption{Point cloud processing pipeline inspired by PointMamba~\cite{liang2024pointmambasimplestatespace}. FPS: Farthest point sampling, kNN: k-Nearest Neighbors.} \label{fig:point-clouds}
elements.sent-2411-121: Our goal is to evaluate the explicit SSM parameterization defined in \secref{STREAM .}
elements.sent-2411-122: Therefore, we align our architecture close to PointMamba~\cite{liang2024pointmambasimplestatespace , which uses the default Mamba module~\cite{gu2024mambalineartimesequencemodeling .}}
elements.sent-2411-123: As visualized in \figref{point-clouds A, we sample $N$ points and sort this point set by the $X$, $Y$, and $Z$ coordinates respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-124: The vector representations extracted from the point coordinates individually are scaled and shifted by learned parameters for the three sorted sequences separately to indicate the sorting dimension.
elements.sent-2411-125: The resulting sequence of $3N$ points is concatenated along the sequence dimension to form the input to our model.
elements.sent-2411-126: This preprocessing simplifies the ordering of the point set by avoiding the two space-filling Hilbert-curves applied by PointMamba at the cost of increasing the input sequence length from $2N$ to $3N$.
elements.sent-2411-127: Similar to \cite{pang2022maskedautoencoderspointcloud, pointgpt23, liang2024pointmambasimplestatespace , our model is composed of two stages.}
elements.sent-2411-128: The input sequence is grouped into sets of \num{32 ~points that are independently processed by a STREAM module to represent local information.}
elements.sent-2411-129: A backbone of \num{12 ~STREAM layers operates on the features extracted from these groups to agglomerate global information.}

elements.sent-2411-130: We evaluate our method on two standardized point cloud classification benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-131: The ModelNet40 dataset~\cite{modelnet40 contains \num{12311 clean point clouds sampled from 3D CAD objects.}}
elements.sent-2411-132: A more realistic dataset of physically scanned indoor scenes is given by ScanObjectNN~\cite{scanobjectnn .}
elements.sent-2411-133: ScanObjectNN contains about \num{15000 scans from \num{2902 unique objects with varying difficulty.}}
elements.sent-2411-134: In both cases, we train our method with the same hyperparameters as PointMamba~\cite{liang2024pointmambasimplestatespace .}
elements.sent-2411-135: We report the best overall classification accuracy, i.e. the average over all samples, obtained from 5 different random seeds.
elements.sent-2411-136: In particular, the hardest instance PB-T50-RS benefits from the exact integration of spatial information and improves PointMamba from \SI{82.48 {\percent to \SI{85.32 {\percent for the best out of 5 random seeds.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-137: With a mean and standard deviation of \SI{84.4+-0.7 {\percent , this poses a significant improvement.}}
elements.sent-2411-138: On ModelNet40, STREAM improves the overall accuracy of PointMamba by \SI{0.3 {\percent from \SI{92.4 {\percent to \SI{92.7 {\percent as reported in \tabref{modelnet40 .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-139: Despite the smaller gain compared to ScanObjectNN, the low variance on this dataset indicates a significant improvement on ModelNet40 as well.
elements.sent-2411-140: The mean and standard deviation of our model is \SI{92.6+-0.1 {\percent .}}
elements.sent-2411-141: Our results show that the inductive bias of explicitly encoding spatial relationships in the SSM parameterization is particularly useful when training models from scratch.

STREAM directly feeds the time coordinate of irregularly spaced events as a parameter to the state-space model as described in \secref{STREAM}, which is indicated by column $t_k - t_{k-1}$. Different variants of STREAM might apply linear transformations and Softplus activations similar to Mamba to adjust the time scales of \(e^{\mathbf{A}\Delta_k}\) (columns $\Delta$) or the factor of $\Gamma_k$ in equation~\eqref{eq:stream2} (columns $\Gamma$). } \label{tab:ablation}
elements.sent-2411-142: In contrast to most prior works, our method directly operates on the stream of events as visualized in \figref{event-based .}
elements.sent-2411-143: The stream is encoded as a sequence of tokens $\func{t_i, \bfu_i}$, where each pixel of the event-based camera is uniquely encoded by a separate token, similar to how text is represented as tokens in modern language modeling~\cite{radford2019language .}
elements.sent-2411-144: This sequence is directly processed by a STREAM module.
elements.sent-2411-145:
To accommodate large streams of up to 
um{131072 events per sample in GPU memory, the output of the first STREAM module is subsampled by a factor of 
um{8 or 
um{16 depending on the task.}}}
elements.sent-2411-146: This way, every event is processed by the neural network at least once directly, in contrast to previous methods that subsampled the raw stream before presenting it to a neural network~\cite{Sekikawa2019, Schaefer2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-147: To create a hierarchical architecture, the sequence is subsampled a second time after half the number of layers.
elements.sent-2411-148: The state dimension is increased by a factor of \num{2 upon the second subsampling.}
elements.sent-2411-149: Our hierarchical subsampling architecture is visualized in \figref{event-based .}
elements.sent-2411-150: We apply average pooling along the sequence dimension before computing the class labels.
elements.sent-2411-151: To improve generalization, we implement a set of geometric data augmentations and a variant of CutMix~\cite{Yun2019cutmix that directly mixes event streams~\cite{Schoene2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-152: We evaluate STREAM on two popular event-based datasets.
elements.sent-2411-153: The DVS128 Gestures dataset~\cite{Amir2017 consists of \num{1342 recordings of 10 distinct classes of hand gestures and an additional class of arbitrary other gestures.}}
elements.sent-2411-154: While this dataset features only a relatively small number of samples for 11 hand gesture categories recorded from 29 subjects, the total number of events in this dataset adds up to about \SI{390 {\million events.}}
elements.sent-2411-155: Our model has an initial model dimension of $n=32$ and a state-space dimension of $m=32$, which is expanded by a factor of \num{2 upon subsampling.}
elements.sent-2411-156: We deploy a total of \num{6 STREAM blocks, which by the notation of \figref{event-based corresponds to $L=2$.}}
elements.sent-2411-157:
We train with a batch size of 
um{32 on sequences of 
um{65536 events such that the total input sequences created by CutMix are up to 
um{131072 events long, and subsample by a factor of 
um{16 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-158: Evaluation is conducted on the full sequences of up to \SI{1.5 {\million events.}}
elements.sent-2411-159: Remarkably, our fully event-based model sets a new state-of-the-art on the DVS128-Gestures dataset as reported in \tabref{gestures , improving over both frame-based and event-based references.}
elements.sent-2411-160: While \cite{Pei2024 reported \SI{100 {\percent accuracy on 10 out of the 11 classes, omitting the `others' class, we for the first time present a model that can reach a maximum accuracy of \SI{100 {\percent on all 11 classes of the dataset.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-161: In addition to event-based vision, we evaluate STREAM on an event-based audio classification task.
elements.sent-2411-162: The Spiking Speech Commands dataset~\cite{Cramer2022 contains more than \num{100000 samples converted from the original Speech Commands dataset~\cite{warden2018speechcommandsdatasetlimitedvocabulary with a median number of \num{8100 events per sample.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-163: The model dimension is again $n=32$ with a smaller state-space dimension of $m=4$ compared to the vision model.
elements.sent-2411-164: We deploy \num{8 STREAM blocks ($L=3$ in \figref{event-based ), and set the subsampling factor to \num{8 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-165: With this configuration, we report a classification accuracy of \SI{86.3 {\percent in \tabref{ssc , which settles between the small and large models reported in~\cite{Schoene2024 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-166: We compare our STREAM module against the Mamba module~\cite{gu2024mambalineartimesequencemodeling in \tabref{ablation .}}
elements.sent-2411-167: The central difference is the explicit integration of spatio-temporal information in the recurrent operator \(e^{\mathbf{A}\Delta_k}\) with $\Delta_k = t_k - t_{k-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-168: We additionally experiment with all combinations of applying linear transformations activated by Softplus functions to renormalize the $\Delta_k$ and $\Gamma_k$ parameters in equations \eqref{eq:stream1 and \eqref{eq:stream2 , respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-169: While the fairly small DVS128 Gestures dataset possesses high variance, STREAM consistently improves over Mamba.
elements.sent-2411-170: This effect is stronger expressed on the larger Spiking Speech Commands dataset, which is less affected by training noise due to the larger number of samples.
elements.sent-2411-171: Here, STREAM creates a significant margin to the Mamba baseline.
elements.sent-2411-172: We have introduced STREAM, a sequence model for point cloud and event stream data, which achieves competitive results on a range of benchmarks from point-cloud classification to event-based vision.
elements.sent-2411-173: Prior efforts of unifying these modalities transferred point cloud models to event streams, or ignore the spatio-temporal structure of either modality by applying the default Mamba model to a sequential view of the data.
elements.sent-2411-174: In contrast, our model exploits the dynamics of state-space models, a temporal process at first sight, to encode spatial geometric information into the models parameterization.
elements.sent-2411-175: This inductive bias proved valuable as STREAM improves our reference model, PointMamba, when trained from scratch on point cloud datasets such as ModelNet40 and ScanObjectNN.
elements.sent-2411-176: By design, STREAM applies to spatio-temporal modalities such as event-based vision.
elements.sent-2411-177: We operate on the stream of events without collapsing events into frames and without using 2D convolutions.
elements.sent-2411-178: This processing paradigm achieves \SI{100 {\percent classification accuracy on all 11 classes of the event-based DVS128 Gestures dataset, a result that has so far only been achieved on the 10 predetermined classes by~\cite{Pei2024 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-179: A practically relevant property of STREAM is that it allows asynchronous inference on streams of points or events recorded from LiDAR sensors or event-based cameras.
elements.sent-2411-180: We, therefore, expect STREAM to be well suited for sensor fusion of these modalities.
elements.sent-2411-181: Previous work has shown that SSMs can benefit significantly from self-supervised pre-training on larger datasets~\cite{liang2024pointmambasimplestatespace , which we will explore for our method in future research.}

elements.sent-2411-191: This section provides complete derivations of equations \eqref{eq:kernel and \eqref{eq:recurrent-ssm .}}
elements.sent-2411-192: We restrict to the case of single-input single-output (SISO) state-space models with multi-dimensional state.
elements.sent-2411-193: Multi-input multi-output (MIMO) formulations like S4~\cite{Gu2022 or Mamba~\cite{gu2024mambalineartimesequencemodeling can be obtained by creating $n$ parallel instances of the SISO model and mixing the input and output components with linear layers.}}
elements.sent-2411-194: Our motivation was to reason about spatial relationships between input pulses.
elements.sent-2411-195: Therefore, we defined an interaction kernel $\bfPhi(\bfx, \bfx^\prime)$ that models pairwise interactions.
elements.sent-2411-196: A complete view of all possible interactions with the point $\bfx_k$ is given by \begin{equation}\bfy\func{\bfx_k} = \int \bfPhi\func{\bfx_k, \bfx^\prime} \bfu\func{\bfx^\prime} d\bfx^\prime \label{eq:appendix-integral-operator} = \sum_{i=0}^N \bfPhi\func{\bfx_k, \bfx_i} \bfu_i\,. \label{eq:appendix-integral-operator-dirac}\end{equation} We will now derive a parameterization of the interaction kernel $\bfPhi$ with a state-space model.
elements.sent-2411-197: A linear time-varying state-space model acting on a scalar input function $u\func{t}$ and producing a scalar output $y\func{t}$ is defined through \begin{equation}\dot{\bfh}\func{t} = \bfA\func{t} \bfh\func{t} + \bfB\func{t} u\func{t} \label{eq:appendix-ssm-h} y\func{t} = \bfC\func{t} \bfh\func{t}\,, \label{eq:appendix-ssm-y}\end{equation} with ${u(t), y(t)\in\mathbb{R }$, states ${\bfh\func{t}\in\R{m }$ and parameters ${\bfA\func{t}\in\R{m\times m , {\bfB\func{t}\in\R{m\times 1 , {\bfC\func{t}\in\R{1\times m }$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-198: Note that we stick to our notation introduced in \secref{methods , denoting vectors and matrices with bold face and scalar values with regular face.}
elements.subsection-2411-010: Solution of the Linear State-space Model
elements.sent-2411-199: Linear ordinary differential equations have a well known analytical solution.
elements.sent-2411-200: We refer the reader to standard calculus textbooks.
elements.sent-2411-201: As such, the linear dynamics of equation \eqref{eq:appendix-ssm-h for initial value $h_0 = h\func{t_0}$ admit the analytical solution \begin{equation}\bfh\func{t} = h_0 + \int_{t_0}^t \exp\func{\int_{t^\prime}^t\bfA\func{t^{\prime\prime}}dt^{\prime\prime}} \bfB\func{t^\prime} u\func{t^\prime} dt^\prime \,, \label{eq:appendix-analytic-solution}\end{equation} which can be checked by taking the derivative of $\bfh$ and comparing it to equation \eqref{eq:appendix-ssm-h .}}
elements.sent-2411-202: Comparing equations \eqref{eq:appendix-analytic-solution , \eqref{eq:appendix-ssm-y and \eqref{eq:appendix-integral-operator , we read off the kernel \begin{equation}\bfPhi\func{t, t^\prime} = \bfC\func{t} \exp\func{\int_{t^\prime}^t\bfA\func{t^{\prime\prime}}dt^{\prime\prime}} \bfB\func{t^\prime}\end{equation} Note that equation \eqref{eq:appendix-analytic-solution can be evaluated on all coordinates $t > t_0$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-203: The two canonical options for discretizing the variables $h$ and $y$ are equidistant steps resulting in a regular grid, or using the input coordinates $t_0, \dots, t_N$.
elements.sent-2411-204: While most state-space model works discretize on equidistant steps, \cite{Smith2023 shows on a toy task that the SSM formulation is capable of solving tasks with irregularly spaced steps as well.}
elements.sent-2411-205: Our work differs from other recent Mamba based models such as PointMamba~\cite{liang2024pointmambasimplestatespace , Point Cloud Mamba~\cite{Tao2024pointcloudmamba , EventMamba~\cite{ren2024rethinkingefficienteffectivepointbased , or SpikMamba~\cite{chen2024spikmambasnnmeetsmamba by integrating the true timings of the inputs in the following sense.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-206: We will use $h_0 = 0$ for notational simplicity in the following.
elements.sent-2411-207: Discretizing equation \eqref{eq:appendix-analytic-solution on the Dirac delta coded input \eqref{eq:input then yields \begin{equation}\bfh\func{t_k} = \int_{t_0}^{t_k} \exp\func{\int_{t}^{t_k}\bfA\func{t^\prime}dt^\prime} \bfB\func{t} \sum_{i=0}^N\delta\func{t - t_i}u_idt \nonumber = \sum_{i=0}^{k} \exp\func{\int_{t_i}^{t_k}\bfA\func{t}dt} \bfB\func{t_i} u_i \,.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-208: We decompose the integral from $t_i$ to $t_k$ into the integrals from $t_{j-1}$ to $t_j$ and sum over them \begin{equation}\bfh\func{t_k} = \sum_{i=0}^{k} \exp\func{\sum_{j=i+1}^{k}\int_{t_{j-1}}^{t_j}\bfA\func{t}dt} \bfB\func{t_i} u_i \,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-209: We will further assume that $\bfA\func{t , \bfB\func{t , \bfC\func{t}$ are constant on the intervals $(t_{j-1 , t_j]$ for $j=i+1, \dots, k$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-210: Denoting ${\bfA\func{t_j = \bfA_j}$, ${\bfB\func{t_j = \bfB_j}$, ${\bfC\func{t_j = \bfC_j}$, ${\bfh\func{t_k = \bfh_k}$, and ${\Delta_j = t_j - t_{j-1 }$ we get \begin{equation}\bfh\func{t_k} = \sum_{i=0}^{k} \exp\func{\sum_{j=i+1}^{k}\bfA_j\Delta_j} \bfB_i u_i = \sum_{i=0}^{k} \left(\prod_{j=i+1}^k\exp\func{\bfA_j\Delta_j}\right) \bfB_i u_i \,. \label{eq:appendix-ssm-discrete}\end{equation} Comparing to equation \eqref{eq:appendix-integral-operator-dirac , we read off the discrete kernel function \begin{equation}\bfPhi\func{t_k, t_i} = \bfC_k \left(\prod_{j=i+1}^k\exp\func{\bfA_j\Delta_j}\right) \bfB_i \,. \label{eq:appendix-kernel-discrete}\end{equation}.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-211: The kernels parameterized by equation \eqref{eq:appendix-kernel-discrete contain convolution operations with rational kernels as a special case.}
elements.sent-2411-212: Consider the linear time-invariant (LTI) case where $\bfA, \bfB, \bfC$ are constant for all $t$.
elements.sent-2411-213: Then \begin{equation}\prod_{j=i+1}^k\exp\func{\bfA_j\Delta_j} = \exp\func{\bfA\func{t_k - t_i}}\end{equation} The proposition follows from the fundamental result from linear systems theory that LTI state-space models can represent every rational transfer function.
elements.subsection-2411-011: Recursive Computation of the Interaction Kernel
elements.sent-2411-214: Let's expand equation \eqref{eq:appendix-ssm-discrete in a recursive form \begin{equation}\bfh_k = \bfh\func{x_k} = \sum_{i=0}^{k} \left(\prod_{j=i+1}^ke^{\bfA_j\Delta_j}\right) \bfB_i u_i = \bfB\bfu_k + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \left(\prod_{j=i+1}^ke^{\bfA_j\Delta_j}\right) \bfB_i u_i =\bfB\bfu_k + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} e^{\bfA_k\Delta_k} \left(\prod_{j=i+1}^{k-1}e^{\bfA_j\Delta_j}\right) \bfB_i u_i =\bfB\bfu_k + e^{\bfA_k\Delta_k} \sum_{i=0}^{k-1}\left(\prod_{j=i+1}^{k-1}e^{\bfA_j\Delta_j}\right) \bfB_i u_i =\bfB\bfu_k + e^{\bfA_k\Delta_k} \bfh_{k-1} \,.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-017: \begin{equation}\bfh_k = \bfh\func{x_k} = \sum_{i=0}^{k} \left(\prod_{j=i+1}^ke^{\bfA_j\Delta_j}\right) \bfB_i u_i = \bfB\bfu_k + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \left(\prod_{j=i+1}^ke^{\bfA_j\Delta_j}\right) \bfB_i u_i =\bfB\bfu_k + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} e^{\bfA_k\Delta_k} \left(\prod_{j=i+1}^{k-1}e^{\bfA_j\Delta_j}\right) \bfB_i u_i =\bfB\bfu_k + e^{\bfA_k\Delta_k} \sum_{i=0}^{k-1}\left(\prod_{j=i+1}^{k-1}e^{\bfA_j\Delta_j}\right) \bfB_i u_i =\bfB\bfu_k + e^{\bfA_k\Delta_k} \bfh_{k-1} \,.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-215: This concludes the derivation of our recurrent operator \begin{equation}\bfh_k =\bfB\bfu_k + e^{\bfA_k\Delta_k} \bfh_{k-1} \,. \label{eq:appendix-recurrent-ssm}\end{equation} In line with most recent SSM works, we choose $\bfA_i \equiv \bfA$ as a diagonal matrix for all $i$.
elements.sent-2411-216: Equation \eqref{eq:appendix-recurrent-ssm allows asynchronous inference on streams of incoming coordinates in $\Order{1}$ time per coordinate, or $\Order{N}$ time for the full stream of coordinates.}
elements.sent-2411-217: Linear time-varying systems such as the one given by equation \eqref{eq:appendix-recurrent-ssm resemble an associative operation, with well-known time complexity of $\Order{\log N}$ \cite{blelloch1990prefix .}}
elements.sent-2411-218: The goal of this section is not to provide a proof, but to give the reader a clear idea of how irrgularly spaced sequences can be parallelized in the same way that regular SSMs can be parallelized with the Scan primitive~\cite{Smith2023, gu2024mambalineartimesequencemodeling .}
elements.sent-2411-220: Consider the pair \begin{equation}\bfc_i = \left[\bfa_i, \bfb_i\right]\end{equation} with the binary operator $\bullet$ defined through \begin{equation}\bfc_i \bullet\bfc_j = \left[ \bfa_i \cdot \bfa_j, \bfa_j \cdot \bfb_i + \bfb_j\right] \,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-221: As~\cite{blelloch1990prefix shows, the operator $\bullet$ is associative, i.e. \begin{equation}\left(\bfc_i \bullet \bfc_j \right) \bullet \bfc_k = \bfc_i \bullet \left( \bfc_j \bullet \bfc_k \right) \,.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-222: Associative operators can be parallelized to run in $\Order{\log N}$ time on sequence of length $N$ given sufficiently many processors~\cite{blelloch1990prefix .}
elements.sent-2411-223: We use this primitive to parallelize our model.
elements.sent-2411-224: The pairs $\bfc$ are initialized as \begin{equation}\bfc_0 = \left[ e^{\bfA\Delta_0}, \bfB_0\bfu_0\right] \quad \dots \quad \bfc_N = \left[ e^{\bfA\Delta_N}, \bfB_N\bfu_N\right]\end{equation} \Figref{scan shows an example of how the Scan primitive computes the entire recurrence in equation \eqref{eq:appendix-recurrent-ssm in roughly $2\log N$ steps.}}
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/2024-11-18-scan.png} \caption{ Example of a Scan operating on four inputs. The left most column represents the input pairs $(e^{\bfA\Delta_i}, \bfB_i\bfu_i)$, and the right most column represents the final results. } \label{fig:scan}
